definition: Representations and warranties (often called 'reps and warranties' or just 'reps') are factual statements made by one party to another as of a specific date — usually the signing date and/or the closing date. If a statement turns out to be false, the party who made it has breached the agreement, which typically triggers indemnification obligations. They serve as a structured due diligence mechanism: the seller (or company) discloses what is true, and the buyer gets a remedy if any disclosure is incorrect.
formalDefinition: Representations are statements of present or past fact. Warranties are promises that a fact is or will remain true. In Canadian commercial practice the two are almost always paired and treated together; a breach of either gives rise to an indemnification claim and, in some cases, a right to terminate.
whenYouSeeThis: Representations and warranties appear in share purchase agreements, asset purchase agreements, subscription agreements, financing documents, and investor rights agreements. They are most extensively negotiated in M&A transactions. In seed-stage financing they are typically shorter and skewed toward the company making statements to investors.
founderNote: Two qualifiers to watch: 'knowledge' (limits your liability to what you actually knew) and 'materiality' (limits your liability to significant breaches). Investors will push to remove both. The disclosure schedule is equally important — whatever you list there is typically carved out from indemnification exposure.
